name: queries
context: Read and write data in SQLite — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CTEs, UPSERT, RETURNING, JSON operators, window functions
info: This skill covers every SQLite query operation an agent needs: selecting data with WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and LIMIT; inserting rows with INSERT and INSERT OR REPLACE; updating with UPDATE and UPDATE OR; deleting with DELETE; using Common Table Expressions (WITH/WITH RECURSIVE) for complex queries; UPSERT via INSERT ON CONFLICT for atomic insert-or-update; RETURNING clauses to get affected rows back; JSON operators (-> and ->>) for querying JSON columns; window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, SUM OVER) for analytics; and parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection. Agents use this skill any time they need to read or write data in a SQLite database. See [[sqlite/schema]] for table design, [[sqlite/performance]] for query optimization, and [[sqlite/extensions]] for FTS5 full-text search queries.
body:
# SQLite Queries

SQLite supports most of SQL-92 plus modern extensions. This skill covers all query operations for reading and writing data.

## SELECT — Reading Data

```sql
-- Basic select
SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE active = 1 ORDER BY name;

-- With LIMIT and OFFSET
SELECT * FROM logs ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50 OFFSET 100;

-- Aggregations
SELECT department, COUNT(*) as count, AVG(salary) as avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING count > 5;

-- DISTINCT
SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products;
```

## JOINs

```sql
-- INNER JOIN
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- LEFT JOIN (include users with no orders)
SELECT u.name, COALESCE(SUM(o.total), 0) as lifetime_value
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
GROUP BY u.id;

-- Self-join (employees with managers)
SELECT e.name, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
```

## INSERT — Writing Data

```sql
-- Single row
INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@example.com');

-- Multiple rows
INSERT INTO tags (name) VALUES ('rust'), ('python'), ('go');

-- Insert from select
INSERT INTO archive (user_id, name, archived_at)
SELECT id, name, datetime('now') FROM users WHERE active = 0;

-- With RETURNING (get inserted data back)
INSERT INTO users (name, email)
VALUES ('Bob', 'bob@example.com')
RETURNING id, name, email;
```

## UPDATE

```sql
-- Basic update
UPDATE users SET active = 0 WHERE last_login < datetime('now', '-1 year');

-- Update with RETURNING
UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.1
WHERE category = 'electronics'
RETURNING id, name, price;

-- Update from another table (using subquery)
UPDATE orders SET status = 'cancelled'
WHERE user_id IN (SELECT id FROM users WHERE banned = 1);
```

## DELETE

```sql
-- Basic delete
DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at < datetime('now');

-- Delete with RETURNING
DELETE FROM temp_files WHERE created_at < datetime('now', '-7 days')
RETURNING id, path;

-- Delete all rows (use TRUNCATE-like pattern)
DELETE FROM logs;
```

## UPSERT — INSERT ON CONFLICT

```sql
-- Insert or update on conflict
INSERT INTO settings (key, value)
VALUES ('theme', 'dark')
ON CONFLICT(key) DO UPDATE SET value = excluded.value;

-- Insert or ignore
INSERT OR IGNORE INTO tags (name) VALUES ('existing-tag');

-- Insert or replace (deletes + re-inserts)
INSERT OR REPLACE INTO cache (key, value, expires)
VALUES ('session:abc', '{"user":1}', datetime('now', '+1 hour'));

-- Upsert with RETURNING
INSERT INTO counters (name, count)
VALUES ('page_views', 1)
ON CONFLICT(name) DO UPDATE SET count = count + 1
RETURNING name, count;
```

## CTEs — Common Table Expressions

```sql
-- Non-recursive CTE
WITH active_users AS (
  SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = 1
),
recent_orders AS (
  SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > datetime('now', '-30 days')
)
SELECT au.name, COUNT(ro.id) as recent_order_count
FROM active_users au
LEFT JOIN recent_orders ro ON ro.user_id = au.id
GROUP BY au.id;

-- Recursive CTE (tree traversal)
WITH RECURSIVE subordinates AS (
  SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS depth
  FROM employees WHERE id = 1
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, s.depth + 1
  FROM employees e
  INNER JOIN subordinates s ON e.manager_id = s.id
)
SELECT * FROM subordinates ORDER BY depth;

-- Recursive CTE (generate series)
WITH RECURSIVE dates(d) AS (
  VALUES(date('now', '-30 days'))
  UNION ALL
  SELECT date(d, '+1 day') FROM dates WHERE d < date('now')
)
SELECT d as date, COALESCE(count, 0) as signups
FROM dates
LEFT JOIN (SELECT date(created_at) as d, COUNT(*) as count FROM users GROUP BY 1) u USING(d);
```

## Window Functions

```sql
-- ROW_NUMBER for pagination
SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY created_at DESC) as row_num
FROM posts;

-- RANK within groups
SELECT name, department, salary,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) as dept_rank
FROM employees;

-- Running total
SELECT date, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) as running_total
FROM transactions;

-- LAG/LEAD for comparing rows
SELECT date, revenue,
  revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) as daily_change
FROM daily_revenue;
```

## JSON Queries

SQLite has built-in JSON support via the `->` and `->>` operators and `json_*` functions:

```sql
-- Extract JSON values
SELECT data->>'$.name' as name, data->>'$.age' as age
FROM profiles;

-- Filter by JSON field
SELECT * FROM events
WHERE data->>'$.type' = 'click'
AND CAST(data->>'$.duration' AS REAL) > 5.0;

-- JSON array operations
SELECT id, json_each.value as tag
FROM posts, json_each(posts.tags);

-- Build JSON responses
SELECT json_object(
  'id', id,
  'name', name,
  'orders', (
    SELECT json_group_array(json_object('id', o.id, 'total', o.total))
    FROM orders o WHERE o.user_id = users.id
  )
) as user_json
FROM users;

-- Modify JSON
UPDATE profiles
SET data = json_set(data, '$.verified', json('true'))
WHERE id = 42;
```

## Parameterized Queries (Prevent SQL Injection)

```python
# Python — ALWAYS use ? placeholders
cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?', (email,))
cursor.execute('INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)', (name, email))
```

```javascript
// Node.js (better-sqlite3) — ALWAYS use ? or $named placeholders
const user = db.prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?').get(email);
const stmt = db.prepare('INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ($name, $email)');
stmt.run({ name: 'Alice', email: 'alice@example.com' });
```

**Never interpolate user input into SQL strings.** Always use parameterized queries.

## Transactions

```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
INSERT INTO accounts (user_id, balance) VALUES (1, 1000);
INSERT INTO ledger (account_id, amount, type) VALUES (1, 1000, 'deposit');
COMMIT;

-- Savepoints for nested transactions
SAVEPOINT batch_insert;
INSERT INTO items (name) VALUES ('item1');
INSERT INTO items (name) VALUES ('item2');
RELEASE batch_insert;
-- or ROLLBACK TO batch_insert; on error
```

## Date and Time

SQLite has no DATE type — store as TEXT (ISO-8601), INTEGER (Unix timestamp), or REAL (Julian day):

```sql
-- Current timestamps
SELECT datetime('now');                    -- 2026-04-11 12:00:00
SELECT strftime('%s', 'now');              -- Unix timestamp
SELECT date('now', '-7 days');             -- 7 days ago

-- Filter by date range
SELECT * FROM events
WHERE created_at BETWEEN datetime('now', '-24 hours') AND datetime('now');

-- Date arithmetic
SELECT date('now', '+1 month', 'start of month', '-1 day') as last_day_of_month;
```

sections.title: SELECT queries
sections.body: Use SELECT with WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, and LIMIT. SQLite supports INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, CROSS JOIN, and self-joins. Use COALESCE for null handling. DISTINCT removes duplicates. Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP_CONCAT, json_group_array.

sections.title: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
sections.body: INSERT single or multiple rows. INSERT from SELECT for bulk operations. UPDATE with WHERE to filter. DELETE with WHERE to target rows. All three support the RETURNING clause to get affected rows back without a separate SELECT. Use parameterized queries (? placeholders) to prevent SQL injection.

sections.title: UPSERT with ON CONFLICT
sections.body: INSERT ON CONFLICT(column) DO UPDATE SET provides atomic upsert. The `excluded` pseudo-table refers to the row that would have been inserted. INSERT OR IGNORE silently skips conflicts. INSERT OR REPLACE deletes the conflicting row then inserts. Upsert is essential for idempotent writes — caches, counters, settings, and sync operations.

sections.title: Common Table Expressions
sections.body: WITH clause creates named temporary result sets. Non-recursive CTEs simplify complex joins and subqueries. Recursive CTEs (WITH RECURSIVE) handle tree traversal, graph walking, and series generation. Recursive CTEs require a base case UNION ALL a recursive step. Use for: org charts, category trees, date range generation, bill-of-materials.

sections.title: Window functions
sections.body: Window functions compute values across rows related to the current row without collapsing them. ROW_NUMBER() for numbering, RANK() for ranking with ties, DENSE_RANK() for gapless ranking. LAG()/LEAD() access previous/next rows. SUM/AVG/COUNT OVER() for running aggregates. PARTITION BY groups windows. ROWS BETWEEN defines the frame. Available since SQLite 3.25.0.

sections.title: JSON queries
sections.body: Use `->` to extract JSON (returns JSON text) and `->>` to extract values (returns SQL type). json_extract() for path-based access. json_each() to iterate arrays. json_group_array()/json_group_object() to build JSON in queries. json_set()/json_insert()/json_replace()/json_remove() to modify JSON. Path syntax: `$.key`, `$.array[0]`, `$.nested.deep`. See [[sqlite/extensions]] for full JSON reference.

sections.title: Date and time functions
sections.body: SQLite has no native DATE type. Store dates as TEXT (ISO-8601 `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS`), INTEGER (Unix epoch), or REAL (Julian day number). Functions: date(), time(), datetime(), julianday(), strftime(), unixepoch(). Modifiers: `'+1 day'`, `'-3 hours'`, `'start of month'`, `'weekday 0'`. Always use `datetime('now')` for current UTC time.

sections.title: Transactions and savepoints
sections.body: BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK for atomic operations. SAVEPOINT/RELEASE/ROLLBACK TO for nested transaction control. In [[sqlite/wal]] mode, readers don't block writers and writers don't block readers. Use `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` if you know you'll write — avoids upgrading a read lock to a write lock mid-transaction. Always handle SQLITE_BUSY with `PRAGMA busy_timeout`.

scripts.name: SQLite Query Operations
scripts.description: Execute common SQLite query patterns: create tables, insert data, query with filters, upsert, CTEs, JSON queries, and transactions

depends.reason: SQLite must be available (sqlite3 CLI or language driver)

author: dojo-community
